data.main_text:
Koe koostuu neljΟΛstΟΛ osasta: kuullunymmΟΛrtΟΛmistehtΟΛvΟΛt, tekstinymmΟΛrtΟΛmistehtΟΛvΟΛt, sanasto- ja rakennetehtΟΛvΟΛt ja kirjallinen tuottaminen.

βÄ΄

Voit tehdΟΛ tehtΟΛvΟΛt haluamassasi jΟΛrjestyksessΟΛ. Noudata tehtΟΛvΟΛkohtaisia vastaamisohjeita. Voit muuttaa antamiasi vastauksia koska tahansa kokeen aikana. Tee kukin tehtΟΛvΟΛ loppuun ennen kuin aloitat seuraavan.

βÄ΄

Vastaa monivalintatehtΟΛviin ja monivalinta-aukkotehtΟΛviin napsauttamalla kulloinkin sopivinta vaihtoehtoa. Kirjoita avoimissa tehtΟΛvissΟΛ vastauksesi tehtΟΛvΟΛn yhteydessΟΛ olevaan vastauskenttΟΛΟΛn. TehtΟΛvissΟΛ voi olla myΟΕs tehtΟΛvΟΛkohtaista ohjeistusta.

βÄ΄

LisΟΛΟΛ kielikokeen vastausohjeita on linkissΟΛ [LINK url=http://yle.fi/plus/abitreenit/2019/syksy/EA-fi/attachments/Kielikokeen-ohjeistus.pdf|text=Kielikokeen ohjeistus].

βÄ΄

KuullunymmΟΛrtΟΛmistehtΟΛvien ΟΛΟΛnitteet ja videot saat kΟΛynnistΟΛΟΛ omaan tahtiisi napsauttamalla siihen liittyvΟΛΟΛ painiketta. Kun olet kΟΛyttΟΛnyt sallitut kuuntelukerrat, et voi enΟΛΟΛ kuunnella ΟΛΟΛnitettΟΛ uudelleen. KΟΛytetyt painikkeet tunnistaa niiden muuttuneesta vΟΛristΟΛ. Varaa aikaa kuullunymmΟΛrtΟΛmistehtΟΛvien tekemiseen yhteensΟΛ noin 1 tunti.

βÄ΄

Videot katsotaan aineistovΟΛlilehdellΟΛ olevalla mediasoittimella. Videoita voit katsoa niin monta kertaa kuin haluat. Voit myΟΕs pysΟΛyttΟΛΟΛ videoiden toiston ja kelata niitΟΛ eteen- tai taaksepΟΛin.

βÄ΄

Joissakin tehtΟΛvissΟΛ on aineistoa, joka lΟΕytyy erilliseltΟΛ vΟΛlilehdeltΟΛ. Sinne pΟΛΟΛset [LINK url=http://yle.fi/plus/abitreenit/2019/syksy/EA-fi/attachments/index.html|text=tΟΛstΟΛ linkistΟΛ]. YksittΟΛisen tehtΟΛvΟΛn aineistoon pΟΛΟΛset myΟΕs suoraan napsauttamalla kyseisen tehtΟΛvΟΛn yhteydessΟΛ olevaa Avaa aineisto -linkkiΟΛ.

βÄ΄

Onnea kokeeseen!

5. YTL

7. YTL

8. https://player.fm/series/series-2281728/the-fastest-woman-in-ireland (15.9.2018).

9. YTL

16. Rianna Croxford, Financial Times, 31 May 2018.

18.3. Image: https://www.siplay.com/ (12.2.2019).

questions.main_text:
**What does music do to you?**

βÄ΄

Music not only affects our mood but also has a beneficial impact on learning, memory, pain alleviation and health in general. βÄ΄

If you wish, you can listen to this while you are writing: [LINK url=http://yle.fi/plus/abitreenit/2019/syksy/EA-fi/attachments/index.html#q18.1|text=Drams Ceilidh Band βÄ™ βÄ€Strip the WillowβÄù]

questions.main_text:
**A Genetic Algorithm**



Read the text carefully and answer the question. Write your answer in Finnish.



The increase of skyscrapers in a city resembles the development of some living systems. Researchers have created an evolutionary genetic algorithm that, on the basis of the historical and economic data of an urban area, can predict what its skyline could look like in the coming years. The method has been applied successfully to the thriving Minato Ward, in Tokyo.



MitΟΛ algoritmilla voi tehdΟΛ? (3 p.)

content.text:

Read the text carefully and answer the question. Write your answer in Finnish.

Researchers have created an evolutionary genetic algorithm that, on the basis of the historical and economic data of an urban area, can predict what its skyline could look like in the coming years. The method has been applied successfully to the thriving Minato Ward, in Tokyo.

questions.main_text:
**Cambridge Pioneer**

βÄ΄

Read the text carefully and for each item choose the alternative that best fits the context. (13 p.)

βÄ΄

I am the first in my family to go to university and faced a lot of the obstacles students of colour encounter when [SELECT id=142] for Oxford and Cambridge. [SELECT id=143]  at state school, I graduated from Cambridge last year as [SELECT id=144] of only seven women of mixed white and black heritage in my year of 3,371 undergraduates.

βÄ΄

As a student I had to deal [SELECT id=145]  different degrees of racism day to day, as well as cultural challenges that my background had not prepared me for. I want to help make [SELECT id=146] easier for other students like myself to enter [SELECT id=147] elite institutions that can offer a fast track to a successful career.

βÄ΄

Getting into Cambridge was a struggle. My first attempt failed. For my A-levels, I had moved from a comprehensive to a grammar school. My A grades at GCSE were considered exceptional at my old school. I was not chosen by the grammar school for Oxbridge preparation sessions or open days. One teacher told me [SELECT id=148]  as she explained: βÄ€ItβÄôs bad for the schoolβÄôs reputation if our candidates donβÄôt get in.βÄù Her words stayed with me. I had never been in the gifted and talented class at [SELECT id=149] school, so maybe she was right, I thought.

βÄ΄

But, ever optimistic, I applied for Cambridge anyway, with no idea what to expect because I did not know anybody who had been there. I secured an interview, for which my Ghanaian mother encouraged me to wear a formal black dress.

βÄ΄

On my way to my interview, I bumped into an applicant from a public school who had just emerged from his on a confident high. βÄ€That was brilliant,βÄù he told me. One of my privately educated friends told me that, at her interview, she simply regurgitated what she had been taught to say in special coaching sessions. According to her, this meant βÄ€getting into Oxbridge was a [SELECT id=150].βÄù

βÄ΄

My own experience could hardly have been more different. Flustered, intimidated and constantly tugging at my dress. I was barely able to think. I managed to waffle about Othello and received pitying looks in return. I was not surprised to [SELECT id=151].

βÄ΄

I received only one university offer that year, from my fifth choice, [SELECT id=152]  I felt would not be worth the ¬Θ30,000 investment. I decided to find a job and reapply to universities later. On my second attempt I got a place.

βÄ΄

Black undergraduates are regularly mistaken [SELECT id=153] tourists or asked to show their ID cards on college grounds.

βÄ΄

The best way to encourage more applicants from diverse backgrounds to aim for Cambridge is by [SELECT id=154] them students like themselves who have succeeded. Mentoring is also critical. There is nothing like hearing a first-hand account from a past student to motivate the hesitant. I mentored three black applicants and two of them received offers at Trinity Hall.

content.text:
**Cambridge Pioneer**

βÄ΄

Read the text carefully and for each item choose the alternative that best fits the context. (13 p.)

βÄ΄

I am the first in my family to go to university and faced a lot of the obstacles students of colour encounter when [SELECT id=142] for Oxford and Cambridge. [SELECT id=143]  at state school, I graduated from Cambridge last year as [SELECT id=144] of only seven women of mixed white and black heritage in my year of 3,371 undergraduates.

βÄ΄

As a student I had to deal [SELECT id=145]  different degrees of racism day to day, as well as cultural challenges that my background had not prepared me for. I want to help make [SELECT id=146] easier for other students like myself to enter [SELECT id=147] elite institutions that can offer a fast track to a successful career.

βÄ΄

Getting into Cambridge was a struggle. My first attempt failed. For my A-levels, I had moved from a comprehensive to a grammar school. My A grades at GCSE were considered exceptional at my old school. I was not chosen by the grammar school for Oxbridge preparation sessions or open days. One teacher told me [SELECT id=148]  as she explained: βÄ€ItβÄôs bad for the schoolβÄôs reputation if our candidates donβÄôt get in.βÄù Her words stayed with me. I had never been in the gifted and talented class at [SELECT id=149] school, so maybe she was right, I thought.

βÄ΄

But, ever optimistic, I applied for Cambridge anyway, with no idea what to expect because I did not know anybody who had been there. I secured an interview, for which my Ghanaian mother encouraged me to wear a formal black dress.

βÄ΄

On my way to my interview, I bumped into an applicant from a public school who had just emerged from his on a confident high. βÄ€That was brilliant,βÄù he told me. One of my privately educated friends told me that, at her interview, she simply regurgitated what she had been taught to say in special coaching sessions. According to her, this meant βÄ€getting into Oxbridge was a [SELECT id=150].βÄù

βÄ΄

My own experience could hardly have been more different. Flustered, intimidated and constantly tugging at my dress. I was barely able to think. I managed to waffle about Othello and received pitying looks in return. I was not surprised to [SELECT id=151].

βÄ΄

I received only one university offer that year, from my fifth choice, [SELECT id=152]  I felt would not be worth the ¬Θ30,000 investment. I decided to find a job and reapply to universities later. On my second attempt I got a place.

βÄ΄

Black undergraduates are regularly mistaken [SELECT id=153] tourists or asked to show their ID cards on college grounds.

βÄ΄

The best way to encourage more applicants from diverse backgrounds to aim for Cambridge is by [SELECT id=154] them students like themselves who have succeeded. Mentoring is also critical. There is nothing like hearing a first-hand account from a past student to motivate the hesitant. I mentored three black applicants and two of them received offers at Trinity Hall.

[LINK url=http://yle.fi/plus/abitreenit/2019/syksy/EA-fi/index.html|text=Kuuntele aineisto tΟΛΟΛltΟΛ]
